content.heading: addEventListener — making elements respond to clicks

blocks.text: addEventListener attaches a function to a DOM element. Every time that element is clicked, the function runs. This is how the Simon Says grid responds when you click a square.

blocks.desc: Every time the element is clicked, call handleClick. Pass the function name without () — you are passing it, not calling it.

lines: // In Simon Says: attach to every grid square

blocks.label: Simon Says connection
blocks.text: Every square in the Simon Says grid has an addEventListener attached to it. When a player clicks a square, it calls handleClick(index) with that square's index number. That index is what gets added to playerClicks.

exercises.title: Attach a click listener
tasks: The page has a button with id='myButton'. Find it.
Write a function called handleClick that logs 'Clicked!'.
Attach it to the button using addEventListener.
Click the button in the preview and confirm the console logs 'Clicked!'.
Now change the function to log how many times the button has been clicked. Declare a counter variable outside the function.
exercises.hint: Declare let count = 0 outside the function. Inside handleClick: count++ then console.log('Clicked', count, 'times').

checks.label: a click listener is attached to myButton

exercises.title: Attach to multiple elements
tasks: The page has four divs with class 'cell' and data-index values 0 through 3.
Use document.querySelectorAll('.cell') to get all four.
Use forEach to attach a click listener to each one.
Each listener should log: 'You clicked cell ' + index.
This is exactly how Simon Says wires up its grid.
exercises.hint: In the forEach callback, use the second parameter (index) to get each element's position: squares.forEach(function(square, index) { ... })

challenges.title: The click listener fires immediately instead of on click
challenges.description: The button should log 'Clicked!' when pressed. But it logs immediately when the page loads and never again. One character is the problem.
challenges.hint: Same rule as setTimeout — no () when passing a function as a callback.
challenges.explanation: handleClick() calls the function immediately and passes its return value (undefined) to addEventListener. Remove the (): addEventListener('click', handleClick). Now the function is passed as a callback and only runs when the button is actually clicked.

challenges.title: All squares log the same index
challenges.description: The grid has 4 squares. Clicking any of them logs 'Cell 3' — always 3, never the correct index. Find the bug.
challenges.hint: This is a classic closure bug. Try using forEach instead — it passes the index as a fresh argument each time.
challenges.explanation: With var or let in a classic for loop, all callbacks share the same 'i' variable. By the time any click fires, the loop has finished and i is 3. Fix: use forEach instead — the index parameter is a fresh copy each time: squares.forEach(function(square, index) { square.addEventListener('click', function() { console.log('Cell', index); }); })
